lessons.title: The Pipeline, and Data as a Table
lessons.content:
In the last sub-topic you learned that machine learning means a computer learns patterns from labeled examples, and that a **model** is like a box of tunable knobs. But how do people actually *build* one of these systems? The good news: there is a recipe, and it is the same recipe almost every time.

**Build.** Building an ML system is a repeatable **pipeline**, a fixed sequence of stages you walk through in order, like following a cooking recipe from prep to plating. The stages, in plain terms, are: **frame the problem** (decide exactly what you want to predict), **get data**, **explore and clean it**, **turn it into features** (the numbers the model reads), **split it into training and test sets**, **pick and train a model**, **evaluate it honestly**, **tune** it, **deploy** it (put it to use), and **monitor** it (watch it over time). Here is the surprise for most beginners: the flashy algorithm is a small part. Most of the real work is the **data** stages, getting it, cleaning it, and shaping it. A common saying is that data work is 80% of the job.

So what does the data actually look like? Picture a **table**, like a spreadsheet. Each **row** is one example, also called a **sample**, say, one fruit, one email, one house. Each **column** is a **feature**, one measured property: a fruit's color, weight, or size. One special column is the **target** (also called the label): the answer you want the model to predict, such as which fruit it is. The features are the inputs; the target is the output you are after.

Features come in a few everyday flavors: **numbers** (a weight like 150 grams), **categories** (a name from a fixed set, like a color or a brand), **text** (words and sentences), and **signals or images** (sound clips, photos). Different flavors need slightly different handling later, but they all end up as rows and columns in a table.

**Recap.** An ML project is a repeatable pipeline, and most of the effort is data, not fancy math. Think of your data as a table: rows are examples, columns are features (the inputs), and one target column holds the answer you want to predict.

items.prompt: In a data table used for machine learning, what does a single ROW usually represent?
choices: One example (a sample), such as one fruit or one email
One feature, such as 'color'
The single answer the model predicts
The name of the whole dataset
items.explanation: Each row is one example, also called a sample, with all of its measured properties laid out across the columns. A single feature like 'color' is a column, not a row, so that choice mixes up the two directions of the table.

items.prompt: In that same table, what do the COLUMNS (other than the target) represent?
choices: The examples, one per column
Only the final answers
Random noise that should be deleted
The features: the measured inputs like weight, color, or size
items.explanation: Columns hold the features, the individual inputs measured for every example, such as weight or color. The examples themselves are the rows, so saying 'one example per column' swaps rows and columns.

items.prompt: What is the 'target' column in a dataset?
choices: A feature that is measured in centimeters
The row at the very bottom of the table
The answer you want the model to predict
The column with the most missing values
items.explanation: The target (also called the label) is the answer you are trying to predict, like which fruit a row describes. It is not just another input feature; it is the specific output the model is being trained to produce.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes building a machine-learning system?
choices: It is a one-step trick: you just run a powerful algorithm once
It is a repeatable pipeline of stages, and most of the work is data
It is mostly about buying the most expensive computer
It has no fixed steps; every project is completely different
items.explanation: ML work follows a repeatable pipeline of stages, and the bulk of the effort goes into getting, cleaning, and shaping data, not the algorithm itself. Treating it as a single magic algorithm step badly underestimates the data work that dominates real projects.

items.prompt: Put these ML workflow stages in the correct order.
choices: Train the model, then get the data, then deploy
Get data, clean it, make features, split, train, evaluate, deploy
Deploy, monitor, then frame the problem
Evaluate, then clean the data, then get the data
items.explanation: The pipeline flows from getting and cleaning data, to building features, to splitting, then training, evaluating, and finally deploying. You cannot train before you have collected and prepared the data, which is why the orders that put training or deploying first are wrong.

items.prompt: Roughly how much of a real ML project tends to be data work (getting, cleaning, and shaping data)?
choices: Almost none; the algorithm is nearly all of it
Exactly half, always
It depends only on the price of the computer
A large majority of the effort
items.explanation: A large majority of real ML effort goes into data, which is why people say data work is around 80% of the job. The flashy algorithm is real but small; assuming the algorithm is nearly everything is the classic beginner misconception.

items.prompt: A column lists each item's color as 'red', 'yellow', or 'orange', chosen from a fixed set of names. What kind of feature is this?
choices: A numerical feature
A categorical feature
The target, automatically
A signal or image feature
items.explanation: A value picked from a fixed set of names, like a color or a brand, is a categorical feature. It is not numerical (the colors are not amounts you can add), and being a category does not by itself make it the target.

items.prompt: What happens during the 'frame the problem' stage of the pipeline?
choices: You decide exactly what you want to predict
You delete all the rows with missing values
You buy a graphics card
You show the finished model to customers
items.explanation: Framing the problem means deciding precisely what question the model should answer, like 'which fruit is this?' Cleaning out missing values and deploying to customers are later stages; they only make sense once you know what you are predicting.

items.prompt: Why is it useful to think of your data as a table with rows and columns?
choices: Because tables make the computer run faster
Because only tables can be stored on a computer
Because it cleanly separates examples (rows) from features (columns) and the target
Because tables remove the need to clean the data
items.explanation: The table picture makes the structure obvious: each row is an example, each column is a feature, and one column is the target you predict. It is an organizing idea, not a speed trick, and it certainly does not remove the cleaning work still to come.

lessons.title: Cleaning and Preparing the Data
lessons.content:
Real-world data is messy. It arrives with gaps, typos, and surprises, and a model that learns from junk will produce junk. There is a famous saying for this: **garbage in, garbage out.** So before any learning happens, you clean and prepare the table.

**Build.** Cleaning means fixing common problems. **Missing values** are blank cells, maybe a fruit's weight was never recorded; you either **fill them in** (for example, with the column's average) or **drop** those rows. **Outliers** are values that look wrong or extreme, like a fruit listed at 50 kilograms; they can mislead the model. **Duplicates** are the same example recorded twice, which over-counts it. **Inconsistent units or scales** cause trouble too: if one row records weight in grams and another in kilograms, the numbers are not comparable until you fix them. And **wrong labels**, an apple mistakenly tagged 'banana', teach the model the wrong answer.

Preparing the data goes a step further, reshaping good data so the model can use it well. First, **scaling**: put features on a **comparable range**. Imagine one column is weight in grams (values in the hundreds) and another is a count like 1 to 5. Without scaling, the big-numbered column can **dominate** simply because its numbers are larger, not because it matters more. Scaling shrinks every feature to a similar range so each gets a fair say. Second, **encoding categories as numbers**: a model does math, so it cannot read the word 'red'. A common fix is **one-hot encoding**: turn one color column into several yes/no columns, one per color (is_red? is_yellow? is_orange?), each holding a 0 or 1. Third, **handling imbalance**: when one class is rare, say 99 'not spam' for every 1 'spam', the model can score well by ignoring the rare class entirely. You correct for this so the rare class still gets learned.

**Recap.** Garbage in, garbage out: clean the data first, fixing missing values, outliers, duplicates, mismatched units, and wrong labels. Then prepare it: scale features to a comparable range so no single column dominates, turn categories into numbers with one-hot encoding, and address class imbalance so rare cases are not ignored.

items.prompt: The phrase 'garbage in, garbage out' in machine learning means what?
choices: The computer physically produces trash
You should always delete every dataset after one use
If you train on messy, bad data, the model's results will be bad too
Garbage cannot be stored in a table
items.explanation: Garbage in, garbage out means the quality of a model is capped by the quality of its data: feed it messy data and you get an unreliable model. It is about data quality driving results, not about literal trash or deleting datasets.

items.prompt: A weight column has some blank cells where the value was never recorded. These are 'missing values'. What are two common ways to handle them?
choices: Fill them in (e.g. with the average) or drop those rows
Always multiply them by zero
Rename the column to hide them
Convert the whole column to text
items.explanation: Missing values are typically either filled in (for instance with the column's average) or the affected rows are dropped. Renaming a column or converting it to text does nothing about the blanks themselves, so those are not real fixes.

items.prompt: One feature is weight in grams (values in the hundreds) and another is a 1-to-5 rating. Why is putting them on a comparable scale important?
choices: So the big-numbered feature doesn't dominate just because its numbers are larger
So the table looks tidier on screen
Because models cannot handle decimals
To permanently delete the smaller feature
items.explanation: Scaling stops a feature with large raw numbers from dominating simply due to its size, giving every feature a fair say. The goal is fairness between features, not tidiness on screen, and scaling keeps every feature rather than deleting any.

items.prompt: A model can only do math with numbers, but one column holds the words 'red', 'yellow', 'orange'. One-hot encoding handles this by doing what?
choices: Deleting the color column entirely
Making several yes/no (0 or 1) columns, one per color
Replacing every color with the number of letters in its name
Translating the colors into another language
items.explanation: One-hot encoding turns a single category column into several 0/1 columns, one for each possible value (is_red? is_yellow? is_orange?). Counting letters would invent meaningless numbers, and deleting the column throws away useful information.

items.prompt: A dataset has 99 'not spam' emails for every 1 'spam' email. Why is this 'class imbalance' a problem?
choices: The model can look accurate by ignoring the rare class entirely
The emails will be stored in the wrong order
Imbalanced data cannot be put in a table
It makes the spam emails disappear from the dataset
items.explanation: With heavy imbalance, a lazy model can always guess the common class and still score high, never truly learning the rare class. The issue is misleading performance on the rare class, not storage order or vanishing data.

items.prompt: Half the rows record weight in grams and half in kilograms. What kind of data problem is this?
choices: Class imbalance
A missing target column
Correct data that needs no attention
Inconsistent units that must be fixed before the numbers are comparable
items.explanation: Mixing grams and kilograms is an inconsistent-units problem: 2 kg and 2 g are wildly different, so the numbers are not comparable until you convert them to one unit. It is unrelated to class imbalance, which is about how often each answer appears.

items.prompt: An apple in the dataset is mistakenly tagged 'banana' in the target column. Why does this 'wrong label' hurt the model?
choices: It makes the file size larger
It changes the color of the row
Wrong labels actually help the model generalize
It teaches the model the wrong answer for that example
items.explanation: Since the model learns from the target answers, a mislabeled example teaches it something false, which degrades what it learns. Wrong labels are harmful noise, never a help, so the idea that they aid generalization is exactly backwards.

items.prompt: The same customer's record appears twice in the table. What is this called, and why does it matter?
choices: A duplicate; it over-counts that example and can bias the model
An outlier; it makes values too large
A missing value; it leaves a blank cell
A feature; it adds a useful new column
items.explanation: A repeated identical record is a duplicate, and it gives that one example extra weight it should not have, skewing what the model learns. An outlier is an extreme value, and a missing value is a blank cell, so neither term fits a repeated row.

items.prompt: A single fruit is recorded as weighing 50 kilograms while all others are around 150 grams. What is this unusual value called?
choices: A label
An outlier
A feature column
A duplicate
items.explanation: A value that is far outside the normal range, like a 50 kg fruit, is an outlier and can mislead the model if left unchecked. It is not a duplicate (a repeated row) nor a label (the answer); it is simply an extreme data point.

items.prompt: Which list correctly groups tasks as CLEANING versus PREPARING the data?
choices: Cleaning: scaling and one-hot encoding. Preparing: fixing typos.
Cleaning and preparing are the same single step with no difference.
Cleaning: missing values, duplicates, wrong labels. Preparing: scaling, encoding categories, fixing imbalance.
Cleaning: training the model. Preparing: deploying it.
items.explanation: Cleaning fixes broken data (missing values, duplicates, wrong labels), while preparing reshapes good data for the model (scaling, encoding, imbalance). Scaling and encoding are preparation, not cleaning, so the option that labels them 'cleaning' has the two backwards.

lessons.title: The Cardinal Rule: No Leakage, and Always Use a Baseline
lessons.content:
Now the single most important lesson in this whole sub-topic. Get this right and you will avoid the mistake that fools more beginners than any other.

**Build.** Recall that you hold out a **test set**, data the model never trains on, to stand in for the **new, unseen data** it will face in the real world. The cardinal rule is simple: **never let the test data influence training.** When information from the test set sneaks into training, that is called **data leakage**, and it quietly ruins everything. Here is the everyday picture: imagine studying for an exam by memorizing the exact questions that will be on it. You will 'ace' your practice run, but you have learned nothing real; the moment a genuinely new exam arrives, you are exposed. Leakage does the same to a model: scores look amazing **in the lab**, then it **fails in the real world**. The practical safeguard: do your cleaning and scaling on the **training data only**, then apply those same settings to the test data. For example, compute the average used to fill missing values from the training set, not from all the data, otherwise the test answers have peeked into training.

The second key idea is the **baseline**. Before you celebrate a fancy model, compare it against something trivial: always guess the most common answer (for a category) or always predict the average (for a number). If your sophisticated model cannot beat that simple baseline, it is not actually learning anything useful. The baseline is your honesty check.

Finally, a note on tools. Libraries like **scikit-learn** give every model the same two simple steps: **fit** (train the model on data) and **predict** (use the trained model on new inputs). You can also chain your cleaning and your model together into one **pipeline** object, which is valuable because it applies each step in the right order and **prevents leakage** automatically. Conceptually, it looks like this:

```
pipeline = clean_and_scale  ->  model
pipeline.fit(training_data)      # learns ONLY from training data
pipeline.predict(new_data)       # use it on unseen data
```

**Recap.** The cardinal rule: test data must never influence training, or you get data leakage, great lab scores and real-world failure, like studying the exact exam answers in advance. Fit cleaning and scaling on the training set only. Always compare against a trivial baseline before trusting a model. And tools give every model the same fit (train) and predict (use) steps, with a pipeline chaining cleaning plus model to keep leakage out.

items.prompt: What is 'data leakage' in machine learning?
choices: When the dataset file is corrupted and won't open
When the model runs slowly on a big dataset
When you lose your data because of a disk failure
When information from the test set sneaks into training
items.explanation: Data leakage is when test-set information improperly influences training, making the model look better than it truly is. It is about information crossing from test into training, not about corrupted files or slow runtimes, which are unrelated technical issues.

items.prompt: Why must the test data never influence training?
choices: Because the test set simulates new, unseen data, and leakage makes lab scores look great but the model fails in the real world
Because the test set is always larger than the training set
Because testing uses a different programming language
Because it makes training run faster
items.explanation: The test set is a stand-in for genuinely new data, so if it leaks into training, your scores are dishonestly inflated and collapse on real inputs. The other options are simply false: the test set is usually smaller, and leakage has nothing to do with speed or languages.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation is the best analogy for data leakage?
choices: Studying by memorizing the exact exam questions beforehand, then 'acing' practice but learning nothing
Forgetting to bring a pencil to the exam
Studying many different practice problems you have never seen the answers to
Taking the exam in a quiet room
items.explanation: Memorizing the actual exam questions gives a fake perfect practice score while teaching you nothing, exactly mirroring how leakage inflates lab results. Studying fresh practice problems with unknown answers is the opposite, the honest way that builds real ability.

items.prompt: You need to fill missing values with a column's average and scale your features. To AVOID leakage, you should compute those settings from which data?
choices: The whole dataset, before splitting
The training data only, then apply the same settings to the test data
The test data only
A random mix chosen fresh each time
items.explanation: You fit cleaning and scaling on the training data alone and then apply those learned settings to the test data, so the test set stays truly unseen. Computing them from the whole dataset lets test information leak in, which is the very mistake to avoid.

items.prompt: What is a 'baseline' in machine learning?
choices: The fastest possible model
The line at the bottom of a chart
A trivial reference, like always guessing the most common answer or predicting the average
The first row of the dataset
items.explanation: A baseline is a deliberately simple predictor, such as always guessing the majority class or the average value, used as a yardstick. It is a comparison point for honesty, not a chart line or a particular data row.

items.prompt: Why should you compare your model against a baseline before trusting it?
choices: Because the baseline trains the model for you
Because baselines make the model run on a phone
Because the baseline picks the features automatically
Because if the model can't beat the trivial baseline, it isn't really learning anything useful
items.explanation: If a fancy model barely matches a trivial guess, it has added complexity without real value, so the baseline exposes that. A baseline is only a yardstick; it does not train the model, choose features, or change where it runs.

items.prompt: In tools like scikit-learn, what do the steps 'fit' and 'predict' mean?
choices: 'fit' deletes data; 'predict' restores it
'fit' and 'predict' both mean the same thing
'fit' trains the model on data; 'predict' uses the trained model on new inputs
'fit' makes a chart; 'predict' prints the table
items.explanation: Across these tools, 'fit' is the training step that tunes the model on data, and 'predict' runs the finished model on new inputs to get answers. They are two distinct stages, so treating them as identical or as charting/printing commands is incorrect.

items.prompt: What is the benefit of chaining your cleaning steps and your model into a single 'pipeline'?
choices: It makes the model bigger on disk
It removes the need to collect any data
It guarantees 100% accuracy every time
It applies each step in the right order and helps prevent leakage automatically
items.explanation: A pipeline runs cleaning then the model in the correct order and refits the cleaning on each training split, which keeps test information from leaking in. It cannot remove the need for data or guarantee perfect accuracy; those claims overstate what a pipeline does.

items.prompt: A model scores 99% on data in the lab but performs terribly once deployed on real, new inputs. What is the most likely culprit a beginner should suspect?
choices: The computer was too fast
The dataset had too many columns to display
Data leakage made the lab score dishonestly high
The model was saved in the wrong folder
items.explanation: A huge gap between glowing lab scores and poor real-world results is the classic fingerprint of data leakage, where the test set secretly influenced training. Hardware speed and file locations do not inflate accuracy, so they are not the cause of this pattern.

items.prompt: For a task where 90% of examples are 'not fraud', a baseline that always guesses 'not fraud' already scores 90%. What does this tell you?
choices: Your model must beat 90% to show it is genuinely learning
90% accuracy from any model is automatically excellent
You should delete the fraud examples
Accuracy can never be measured here
items.explanation: Because a trivial guess already reaches 90%, a real model has to do better than that baseline to prove it is learning the rare fraud cases. Concluding that 90% is automatically excellent is the trap; on imbalanced data the baseline sets a high bar that accuracy alone can hide.
